frozen_components: supervised feature dictionary (fixed by attribute averaging)
architecture.summary: The paper proposes a three-stage evaluation framework for sparse autoencoders (SAEs) applied to language model interpretability. First, supervised feature dictionaries are constructed from task-relevant attributes (e.g., subject, indirect object, position in IOI task) by averaging activations conditioned on attribute values. These serve as a reference benchmark. Second, unsupervised SAEs are trained on either task-specific (IOI) or full-distribution (OpenWebText) data to learn sparse decompositions of model activations. Third, both supervised and unsupervised dictionaries are evaluated along three axes: (1) sufficiency/necessity of reconstructions for task performance via activation intervention; (2) sparse controllability via greedy feature-edit optimization to achieve counterfactual outcomes; (3) interpretability via F1-scoring of feature activations against attribute-value properties and causal validation. The system operates at cross-sections of the IOI circuit in GPT-2 Small, reading and intervening on attention head outputs and queries.
trained_components: sparse autoencoder encoder and decoder (task-specific SAEs on IOI dataset)
sparse autoencoder encoder and decoder (full-distribution SAEs on OpenWebText)

biblio.title: Towards Principled Evaluations of Sparse Autoencoders for Interpretability and Control

claims.statement: Supervised feature dictionaries achieve excellent approximation, control, and interpretability of model computations on the IOI task
Supervised feature dictionaries approximate activations using as few as 3 active features per activation
Any of the IO, S, and Pos attributes can be edited precisely by replacing only 1 active feature with a different one using supervised dictionaries

Task-specific SAEs offer a significantly worse approximation compared to the supervised feature dictionaries on the sufficiency/necessity test; full-distribution SAEs fare notably worse than task-specific SAEs

SAEs are less successful than supervised features in controlling the model; task-specific SAEs can edit the Pos attribute and to a small extent the IO attribute, but require changing more features compared to supervised dictionaries
Full-distribution SAEs require a significantly larger number of features to achieve statistically significant control compared to task-specific SAEs, often 32 or more features
Feature occlusion occurs in SAE training where a causally relevant concept is robustly overshadowed by even slightly higher-magnitude ones in the learned features
Feature over-splitting occurs in SAE training where binary features split into many smaller, less interpretable features
